summary: Mexican feminist author and poet. Works include The Book of Lamentations, Ciudad Real, The Eternal Feminine, and the poem 'Valium 10.' Directed puppet plays for the Maya. Wrote 'In Memory of Tlatelolco.'

works.name: The Book of Lamentations
works.description: Novel set in Chiapas about Maya-Mexican tensions.

clues.clue: Maya rebels crucify a child and drink his blood to become invulnerable to bullets
examples: Rebels of this ethnicity crucify a child and drink his blood

works.name: General
works.description: Feminist work, Tlatelolco poem, puppet plays for Maya.

clues.clue: Posthumous play The Eternal Feminine imagines conversation between notable Mexican women including Sor Juana
examples: posthumous play The Eternal Feminine imagines a conversation between notable women in Mexican history

clues.clue: Poem 'In Memory of Tlatelolco' ends 'I remember, we remember / until we feel justice at last here among us'
examples: A poem commemorating this event ends 'I remember, we remember'

clues.clue: Poem 'Valium 10' — confessional poem about 'measuring stick breaks' and 'compass goes missing'
examples: described how sometimes your 'measuring stick breaks' and 'your compass goes missing' in her confessional poem 'Valium 10'

clues.clue: Directed puppet plays for the Maya (Monkey Business Theater)
examples: directed puppet plays for this ethnic group, who lead the Monkey Business Theater

comprehensive_summary: Rosario Castellanos (1925-1974) was a pioneering Mexican feminist author and poet. The Book of Lamentations is set in Chiapas and depicts Maya-Mexican tensions, including a scene where Maya rebels crucify a child. Her posthumous play The Eternal Feminine imagines conversations between notable Mexican women. She wrote 'In Memory of Tlatelolco' commemorating the 1968 massacre and the confessional poem 'Valium 10.' She directed puppet plays for the Maya.

cards.front: Novel: set in Chiapas; Maya rebels crucify a child and drink his blood to become invulnerable to bullets
Author: posthumous play imagines conversation between notable Mexican women including Sor Juana; wrote confessional poem 'Valium 10'
